attack_chain.description: Uber engineer inadvertently posted an AWS access key in a public GitHub Gist (a data analysis script) in March 2014. The key remained publicly accessible for months.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: A copy of the key was inadvertently posted by Uber on one of its public pages on the code development platform GitHub in March of 2014

evidence_chunks.quote: A copy of the key was inadvertently posted by Uber on one of its public pages on the code development platform GitHub in March of 2014
A copy of the key was inadvertently posted by Uber on one of its public pages on the code development platform GitHub in March of 2014

attack_chain.description: Adversary discovered the AWS credential by visiting the public GitHub Gist page containing the data analysis script.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: An adversary discovered an Uber credential that was leaked in a GitHub Gist.

evidence_chunks.quote: An adversary discovered an Uber credential that was leaked in a GitHub Gist.
An adversary discovered an Uber credential that was leaked in a GitHub Gist.

attack_chain.description: Adversary pivoted from the initial Comcast IP to use a Scandinavian VPN service to anonymize traffic prior to accessing AWS.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: the address from which the hack was launched is associated with a virtual private network service ... based in a Scandinavian country

attack_chain.description: Adversary used the leaked Uber AWS access key to authenticate to the AWS API as a valid Uber cloud identity.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: someone had used an Uber digital security key to access the driver database

evidence_chunks.quote: someone had used an Uber digital security key to access the driver database
someone had used an Uber digital security key to access the driver database

attack_chain.description: Adversary enumerated S3 buckets/objects to identify database prune files stored in S3.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: exfiltrate several objects containing database prunes held in an S3 bucket

evidence_chunks.quote: exfiltrate several objects containing database prunes held in an S3 bucket
exfiltrate several objects containing database prunes held in an S3 bucket

attack_chain.description: Adversary downloaded database prune objects from the S3 bucket containing ~50,000 records including names and driver's license numbers.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: exfiltrate several objects containing database prunes held in an S3 bucket on 2014–05–12 ... These S3 objects contained about 50,000 records, including names and driver's licenses.

attack_chain.description: Adversary exfiltrated the downloaded S3 objects out over the internet (via VPN) to their own infrastructure.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: to exfiltrate several objects containing database prunes held in an S3 bucket on 2014–05–12

initial_access_vector: Leaked AWS access key in a public GitHub Gist (Uber data analysis script), discovered by the adversary and used to call the AWS API.
persistence_mechanism: None documented; access persisted only through continued validity of the leaked static AWS credential which was not rotated.
privilege_escalation: None; the leaked credential already had sufficient privileges to read the S3 bucket containing database prunes.
